text:
From: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.inference>
To: Emily Chen <emily@ezpay.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.inference>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.inference>
Date: Fri, 10 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Anchored breakpoints & graduated rebate proposal for EzPay

Hi Emily —

Thanks again for the conversation yesterday. As discussed, I put together a lightweight proposal that tries to balance EzPay's ask for predictability and your desire to capture upside. The attached PDF (EZPay_pricing_proposal_v1.pdf) contains the full table; below is the executive summary and a suggested mutual action plan.

Executive summary
- Baseline per-token list rate: $0.00045 (hosted API, text-generation)
- Anchored breakpoints (quarterly aggregated):
  * Tier A: 0 - 50M tokens -> baseline rate
  * Tier B: 50M - 200M tokens -> marginal rebate 12%
  * Tier C: 200M+ tokens -> marginal rebate 22%
- Transitional smoothing credit: for Q1 post-commit, we apply a retroactive blended credit up to 6% if you ramp slower than forecast (protects you vs. step cliffs)
- Overage treatment: marginal overage rebate capped at 15% (to avoid runaway discounts)

Why this structure? It preserves marginal economics (you only get the higher rebate for incremental tokens beyond a breakpoint) while adding an anchored smoothing credit so billing is readable for your finance team. This is a middle ground between pure blended and pure marginal approaches.

Next steps / asks
1) Internal confirmation: can procurement accept retroactive credits applied as invoice adjustments?
2) Would EzPay prefer rebates applied retroactively (credit back on invoice) or as upfront monthly bill-credit?
3) Can we aim for an LOI signature within the next two weeks to lock pricing for calendar Q3?

Proposed mutual action plan (high-level)
- Week 0: Aisha shares v1 proposal (attached)
- Week 1: Procurement review & confirm billing approach; Redwood legal to prepare LOI
- Week 2: Sign LOI; schedule technical kickoff

Attaching: EZPay_pricing_proposal_v1.pdf with the breakpoint tables and examples. Happy to hop on a short call to walk through the math.

Best,

Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
<aisha@redwood.inference>
+1 (415) 555-0142

Attachments: EZPay_pricing_proposal_v1.pdf


From: Emily Chen <emily@ezpay.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha@redwood.inference>
Cc: EzPay Procurement <procurement@ezpay.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Apr 2027 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Anchored breakpoints & graduated rebate proposal for EzPay

Aisha — thanks for the quick turn. I read the proposal and the structure makes sense in principle but our finance team has a few practical asks before we can move forward: 

1) Simplicity: our billing ops prefers either a simple blended effective rate applied monthly or a very clearly documented marginal process. The mix with a transitional smoothing credit is new to them — can you show a sample invoice and two worked examples (one slow ramp, one fast ramp)?
2) Overage concern: if we spike in usage for a short period (e.g., product launch), will we be penalized by lower rebates vs. a blended model?
3) Commitment term: is the proposal tied to a 12-month commitment? What flexibility do we have to re-open thresholds mid-term if market/usage changes?

Also looping in procurement@ezpay.com for contract/PO questions. If you can share a one-page summary and the sample invoice examples, that would help speed our internal review.

Best,
Emily Chen
Head of Platform, EzPay
emily@ezpay.com

Folks — forwarding Emily's reply (excerpted below). Procurement is worried about complexity and overage spikes. Can we propose a defensible bridging approach that keeps margin integrity for Redwood but addresses their "simplicity" ask?

Key asks from Emily/procurement:
- Provide two worked invoice examples (slow ramp vs fast ramp)
- Clarify how overage spikes are treated vs blended alternatives
- Confirm term flexibility (can they re-open thresholds if usage materially deviates)

My suggested options to evaluate: 
A) Retroactive marginal with a 90-day smoothing bridge: rebates applied retroactively but we provide a bill credit smoothing buffer that attenuates a step cliff in the first quarter.
B) Hybrid monthly blended floor + marginal upside: apply a floor blended rate for monthly billing and then true-up quarterly with marginal rebates (this makes monthly invoices predictable).
C) Simplified blended option: offer a blended effective rate at an agreed forecast with a runway for re-negotiation at 6 months. Lower upside but simplest for ops.

Please review and annotate in-line. I want to get back to Emily with a clear recommendation and sample numbers by EOD Monday.

Thanks,
Aisha

title: Mutual Action Plan — Peak run scheduling & dedicated capacity allocation (FastBank)

Cc: Aaron Liu <aaron.liu@fastbank.com>
Date: Mon, 12 Apr 2027 09:04:00 -0400
Subject: Follow-up: load/perf MAP items + target windows

Ben — thanks for the call this morning. Quick recap and asks so we can lock the MAP milestone for the load validation runs: 

Summary of ask
- FastBank wants a traffic replay run to validate peak QPS (target: 6k qps sustained for 30m, with 10k qps bursts up to 5m).
- We need Redwood to size Dedicated capacity options, provide a canary plan, and confirm KV/prefix cache behavior during the run.

Preferred windows
- Dry run (canary): Apr 28 02:00–04:00 UTC
- Full replay: Apr 30 01:00–05:00 UTC

Dependencies on our side
- We will provide anonymized traffic sample and a redaction checklist by Apr 16.
- Procurement needs a signed SOW or confirmed pricing by Apr 20 to start provisioning dedicated capacity.

Deliverables we expect from Redwood
- MAP milestone sheet with owners, dates, and acceptance criteria
- Capacity-sizing options (hourly/monthly) and recommended fallback models
- Ops runbook for the replay (checklist + rollback steps)

Attachments: I can share a small traffic sample but will wait for your secure upload link.

Next steps: please send a MAP draft with milestone owners and a proposed pricing commit cadence by Apr 14 so we can route internally. CC'ing Aaron from engineering for scheduling constraints.

Thanks,
Danielle
Danielle Rios
Director, Platform Reliability
FastBank



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Danielle Rios <drios@fastbank.com>

Subject: Re: Follow-up: load/perf MAP items + target windows

Danielle — appreciated the clear summary. Attached is a first-pass MAP draft (MAP_draft_v1.xlsx) that breaks the milestone into three concrete checkpoints and owner names. Highlights below: 

MAP highlights (high level)
- Milestone A (Prep & Access) — owner: FastBank (Danielle/Aaron). Due: Apr 16. Deliverables: anonymized traffic sample, test credentials, signed AUP.
- Milestone B (Canary Dry Run) — owner: Redwood (Karthik). Planned: Apr 28 UTC window. Acceptance: < 20% error, p50 latency < 120ms.
- Milestone C (Full Replay) — owner: Redwood + FastBank. Planned: Apr 30 UTC. Acceptance: sustained 6k qps baseline, successful canary criteria met.

Capacity & pricing notes
- We included three sizing options in the attached PDF: Conserved (50% headroom), Standard (100% headroom), Aggressive (200% headroom). We also noted model variants (fp16 vs 8-bit) and their perf tradeoffs.
- Procurement timeline: to hit Apr 28 canary we need purchase commitment by Apr 20 to provision dedicated nodes.

Ops runbook
- Karthik is drafting the runbook; we'll share a read-only Drive folder with runbook + playbook by Apr 20.

Security/compliance
- If you need additional evidence (SOC 2 artifact), we can attach the SOC attestation; for Private deployment customers we surface audit logs as part of the package.

Attachments: MAP_draft_v1.xlsx, capacity-sizing-options.pdf (see the 'Notes' tab for model-specific assumptions).

Proposed next steps
1) Danielle/Aaron: confirm readiness dates and upload sample traffic to the secure folder by Apr 16.
2) Procurement: confirm whom to invoice / PO cadence by Apr 20.
3) Redwood (Karthik): circulate canary runbook draft by Apr 20 and the traffic-specs.json by Apr 21.

I'll follow up with a quick call invite to align slots for the canary.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference



From: Aaron Liu <aaron.liu@fastbank.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Danielle Rios <drios@fastbank.com>
Cc: procure@fastbank.com
Date: Mon, 12 Apr 2027 13:02:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Follow-up: load/perf MAP items + target windows

Ben / Danielle — quick confirmation from engineering: the Apr 28 and Apr 30 windows look feasible on our side. A couple of asks from ops/security: 

- We need the canary runbook to include explicit rollback commands and a verification checklist for redaction.
- Please confirm whether the dedicated capacity nodes will be in our region (us-east-1) and whether cross-region fallback is possible if we see perf regressions.
- Procurement: can you provide an estimate of one-off setup fees vs monthly committed spend? That will determine our PO schedule.

Danielle, I loop procurement in. Ben — can you get those three items into the MAP?

Thanks,
Aaron
Platform Engineering Manager
FastBank



From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Aaron Liu <aaron.liu@fastbank.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Danielle Rios <drios@fastbank.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 12 Apr 2027 17:22:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Follow-up: load/perf MAP items + target windows

Aaron / Danielle — adding technical detail and recommended ops controls so the MAP has clear acceptance criteria: 

Sizing recommendation (initial)
- Standard plan (recommended): 12 x A100-equivalent workers with NVMe local cache, expected sustained throughput ~6k qps with model variant V2-fp16.
- Burst plan: auto-burst to additional 4 nodes for short 5m spikes (10k qps).
- If you choose quantized 8-bit variant (V2-8q), same baseline can be achieved with 8 workers but expect slight increases in 95th pct latency (15–25ms).

Runbook & safe-guards
- We'll provide a dry-run checklist and explicit rollback steps (scale down policy + route traffic back to canary model).
- KV/prefix cache behavior: we'll pre-warm prefix caches during the canary; full-replay will use warmed caches to avoid cold-cache latencies.

Operational asks from FastBank
- Provide an admin test account with representative auth scopes (token expiry >= 60m) by Apr 16.
- Confirm the list of IPs to allow in our dedicated subnet by Apr 20.

Attachments: traffic-specs.json (sample format), capacity-sizing-options.pdf (technical appendix).

I recommend we schedule a 30-minute prerehearsal on Apr 26 to walk through the runbook and smoke-test the upload of the traffic file. If that works, Ben can send a calendar invite.

Cheers,
Karthik Iyer
Head of Inference Engineering | Redwood Inference

title: SummitCare: temporary quarterly invoicing + net-60 request
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Dana Morales <accounts-payable@summitcare.org>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: quarterly invoicing pilot + net-60 (SummitCare)

Hi Dana —

Thanks again for the thoughtful conversation this morning. Per SummitCare finance, they want to move to a quarterly invoicing cadence aligned to their internal consolidation dates for the next two quarters (May and August invoices). Their ask:

- Quarterly invoices (one invoice covering the quarter) starting 2027-05-01 for Q2 and Q3 as a pilot.
- Net-60 payment terms from invoice date (our standard is Net-30).
- If Redwood accepts quarterly invoices, SummitCare would prepay 30% of each quarterly invoice within 7 days of receipt; they are also requesting a 10% early-payment credit on prepaid amount as an incentive.

From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Thu, 8 Apr 2027 11:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: quarterly invoicing pilot + net-60 (SummitCare)

Ben — quick internal summary / risk note before we escalate: 

- Customer history: SummitCare is an existing customer (since 2025) on standard monthly billing. Usage has grown ~20% q/q; ARR footprint ~ $600k.
- Risk: moving to quarterly invoicing increases exposure window significantly; Net-60 compounds that. If they prepay 30% that mitigates but does not eliminate consumption risk.
- Operational impact: quarterly invoices require PSR change and a different invoice identifier for our billing system; ops says 5 business days to implement once approved.
- Suggested mitigations we recommend to leadership:
  * Limit pilot to two quarters (Q2 & Q3 2027), automatic reversion to monthly afterward.
  * Require 30% prepayment as stated, funds cleared before license/overage invoicing considered satisfied.
  * Cap total Redwood exposure at $300k outstanding — any usage above is invoiced monthly.
  * Approve a 10% credit only applied to the prepaid portion, not to the net outstanding balance.
  * Use escrow or dedicated remittance instructions for amounts > $250k — legal to draft simple addendum.

If leadership is comfortable, we should accept conditionally and sign a 2-page addendum that covers: term, restart to monthly, escrow clause, and the early-pay credit mechanics.

—Monica

Redwood Inference

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>

Subject: Fwd: Request: quarterly invoicing pilot + net-60 (SummitCare) — formal approval requested

Jonas / Marissa — forwarding the ask and Monica’s recommendations. Customer needs a decision this week so they can align treasury for May 1 start. My commercial ask summarized: 

Proposal for approval (one-liner): Redwood to accept temporary quarterly invoicing + Net-60 for two quarters, conditioned on 30% prepayment, a cap on outstanding exposure, and a 10% credit applied only to advance/prepaid portion.

Detailed terms we need to approve (for Deal Desk signoff):
1) Term: Pilot limited to Q2 & Q3 2027, auto-revert to monthly unless both parties agree in writing.
2) Payment: Quarterly invoice sent 1st business day after quarter close; Net-60 on invoice date. 30% of each invoice to be prepaid within 7 days; unpaid prepaid portion voids credit.
3) Credit: 10% early-payment credit only applied to the prepaid 30% amount (not to total invoice).
4) Exposure/cap: Redwood exposure cannot exceed $300k undisputed outstanding. If usage would push beyond $300k, additional charges billed monthly.

Thanks,
Ben

To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>

Date: Sun, 11 Apr 2027 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Request: quarterly invoicing pilot + net-60 (SummitCare) — formal approval requested

Ben / Marissa — after quick review with Finance, we can approve this as a limited pilot with the following mandatory conditions (Finance & Legal):

1) Pilot limited to Q2 & Q3 2027 (auto-revert to monthly) — APPROVED.
2) 30% prepayment must be cleared in our account before the quarter is considered paid; we will not extend services withheld for non-payment but will flag usage for potential suspension if exposure > $300k — APPROVED.
3) 10% early-payment credit may be offered but it must be capped: credit applies only to the prepaid 30% and maximum credit per quarter capped at $5,000 (to limit precedent).
4) Exposure: total outstanding undisputed exposure capped at $300k — APPROVED.
5) Escrow: for any single quarterly invoice > $250k, require funds in an approved escrow account or wire confirmation; Legal to prepare a short addendum capturing remittance mechanics.
6) Accounting/Revenue recognition: RevRec to confirm but Finance flags that the 30% prepay will be treated as deferred revenue until services are delivered; ensure Accounting signs off before sending the addendum.

Jonas asks Legal to draft a one-page addendum capturing the above; Deal Desk: once Legal posts the addendum to the drive link, Ben can send to SummitCare for signature.

Decision: CONDITIONAL APPROVAL (Finance + Legal conditions above).

Jonas Weber
Head of Finance, Revenue Operations
Redwood Inference

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood-inference.com>

Date: Fri, 9 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: POC Kickoff — Hands-on session: access, routing & load plan

Hi Alex —

Thanks again for planning the POC with LedgerLane. To make the hands-on session useful for both teams, proposed plan below and a couple of logistics questions so we can lock the calendar: 

Proposed format (3 hours total)
- 09:30–10:00 Preflight & prompt review (share prompt corpus + expected outputs)
- 10:00–11:00 Deep dive: routing and prompt routing rules + KV cache walkthrough
- 11:00–12:00 Load validation & throughput tests (we’ll run synthetic traffic and a burst test)

Two scheduling options: onsite (Redwood team at your San Francisco office) or hybrid (Redwood remote join, LedgerLane attendees onsite). Dates we have open: Apr 22 or Apr 28 (both workdays). Please confirm preferred date and whether you want Redwood onsite. 

Logistics ask (please confirm):
- Final attendee list (names + emails) by Apr 15 so Marissa can secure visitor badges
- Any corporate firewall/nat egress rules that would block streaming audio/video or our test harness (we’ll provide IP ranges)
- Recording permission: do you permit the session to be recorded for internal playback? We can record only the Redwood screens if preferred.
- AV & screen sharing: for onsite, will we have a laptop with HDMI, or should we bring our own?

I attached a short preflight checklist and a visitor pass template to make approvals easier. If you prefer a dry-run (30 min) the week prior, we can schedule that too.

Thanks —

Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood-inference.com | +1 415-555-0198

Attachments:
- preflight-checklist.pdf (1 page)
- visitor-pass-template.pdf (stub)

Subject: Request: network termination and tenant egress model for SOC 2 review

Hi Ben,

We're kicking off our SOC 2 readiness review for the project using Redwood Dedicated and Private deployments. Can you provide:

- A short architecture summary showing where tenant traffic terminates at the edge and how egress is enforced for Dedicated vs Private.
- A redacted network diagram suitable for sharing with auditors (no customer IPs).
- CAIQ/SIG mapping for the controls related to network segregation, egress filtering, and KMS boundary.
- Any evidentiary artifacts (config snippets, Netflow / flow logs examples, and a short attestation statement).

We prefer a single ZIP with a README and diagram PDF. Is early next week feasible? We're aiming to close this segment by April 20.

Thanks,
Anil
Security Lead, Veridian Cloud



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anil Gupta <anil.gupta@veridian.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Apr 2027 07:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Request: network termination and tenant egress model for SOC 2 review

Hi Anil — thanks for the note.

Short plan:

- I'll assemble a redacted diagram + a one-page summary describing the 'edge termination patterns' we use for Dedicated (customer-dedicated pools, NAT gateways, per-tenant security groups) and Private (VPC peering, private control plane, optional airgap).
- Kimberly will prepare the CAIQ/SIG control mapping and the suggested excerpts from flow logs for evidence.
- Marissa will produce the README and an export-ready PDF.

Deliverables (target):
1) redwood-silo-termination-diagram.pdf (redacted)
2) control-mapping-soc2-sig.xlsx
3) egress-sample-flows.txt (sanitized)
4) attestation-edge-egress.txt

I'll upload to a shared link and send you the ZIP by Friday (April 9). If you need a walkthrough with the audit team, propose a 30-min slot and we'll join.

Best,
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachments referenced: redwood-termination-sketch.svg (internal)

To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>

> 
> Short plan:
> 
> - I'll assemble a redacted diagram + a one-page summary describing the 'edge termination patterns' we use for Dedicated...

Internal note / instructions (do not share raw configs):
- Redaction: strip customer CIDRs and any tenancy IDs from diagrams. Use synthetic prefixes for sample flows (10.0.0.0/16 style).
- Evidence: provide a flow log excerpt with timestamps and sanitized src/dst fields + the control-mapping excel that ties SIG/CAIQ questions to our implemented controls (e.g., SIG:SC-3 -> per-tenant SGs + NAT egress policing).
- KMS boundary: include the short attestation that key material for customer-managed keys (if used) is scoped to the private deployment and not exported to hosted control plane logs.
- Suggested attestation wording (one-liner) for auditors: "Redwood Inference confirms that tenant network egress from Dedicated and Private deployments is enforced by per-tenant security groups and centralized egress filtering; redacted logs provided demonstrate enforced deny-by-default policies."

I'll draft the CAIQ mapping and a short attestation; Marissa please prepare the redacted PDF and ZIP layout (README, diagram, control-mapping, sanitized flows, attestation).

Kimberly
Director, Security Architecture



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anil Gupta <anil.gupta@veridian.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 9 Apr 2027 15:24:00 -07:00
Subject: Deliverable: Tenant termination patterns and edge-silo model — SOC2 & SIG deliverables

Anil — attaching the ZIP with the artifacts we discussed. Contents: README.md, redwood-silo-termination-diagram.pdf (redacted), control-mapping-soc2-sig.xlsx, egress-sample-flows.txt (sanitized), attestation-edge-egress.txt.

Download (expiring link): https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1Xk-example-drive-link
HubSpot: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/47291/deal/47291

Quick summary:
- Termination pattern: Dedicated uses customer-dedicated GPU pools with tenant-specific security groups and NAT egress gating; Private uses VPC peering and a customer-controlled routing plane. Both support deny-by-default egress with centralized firewall rules enforced at the ingress/egress layer.
- Evidence provided: redacted diagram, control mapping to SIG/CAIQ controls for network segregation and egress, a sanitized Netflow excerpt showing enforced deny results, and an explicit attestation statement signed by our Security Director (Kimberly Park).

If the Veridian audit team wants a 30-min walkthrough, suggest Monday Apr 12 at 10am PT or Tue Apr 13 at 2pm PT.

Thanks,
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

preview:
From: Alice Hansen <alice.hansen@evergreenbank.com>

Subject: Redwood DPA addendum / subprocessor list + transfer mechanism

Ben —

Thanks for taking our call this morning. Evergreen is getting close to a formal procurement decision, and compliance asked me to pull together a short checklist so we can close the legal review. Can you confirm the following items and provide any supporting docs?

- Latest subprocessor inventory (including country of processing and subprocessors that handle logs/KV caches)
- Suggested DPA addendum wording for EU/UK data transfers (we're US-based but have EU cardholders)
- SOC 2 report and attestation (redacted if needed)
- Confirmation of audit rights (on-site vs. remote; expected lead time) and a contact for SOC/audit scheduling
- DPIA support: can Redwood provide a short statement on processing categories and risk mitigation for our DPIA, and a contact to coordinate evidence?

We're on a tight timeline — procurement would like this in the next 5 business days. If some items require an NDA or data sharing agreement, let me know and we'll expedite.

Thanks,
Alice Hansen
Senior Procurement Manager
Evergreen Bank
alice.hansen@evergreenbank.com

--

To: Alice Hansen <alice.hansen@evergreenbank.com>

Date: Thu, 8 Apr 2027 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Redwood DPA addendum / subprocessor list + transfer mechanism

Hi Alice —

Appreciate the clear checklist. Quick status and next steps below; attaching the items we can share immediately.

What I'm attaching now:
- redwood_subprocessors_list_20270407.csv (includes subprocessors, country, and brief processor role)
- SOC2-report-redwood-2026.pdf (redacted control IDs)
- DPA-addendum-draft-evb-v1.docx (proposed addendum language we typically use for banking customers)

On the outstanding asks:
- Audit rights: Redwood supports remote audit evidence requests and scheduled on-site audits with reasonable advance notice. Typical remote evidence turnaround is 5-10 business days. If you need a dedicated on-site window we can coordinate via our ComplianceOps team (contact in doc).
- DPIA support: we can provide a DPIA support statement plus a short implementation note that summarizes processing categories, data flows, KV caching behaviors, and mitigations (encryption at rest/in transit, token-level access controls, retention policies). Priyom (cc'd) owns DPIA support and will provide the statement and a sample mapping.
- Transfers: for EU-related processing we offer SCCs + redwood-hosted transfer safeguards; if you need onshore-only hosting we can propose a dedicated environment (pricing differs). The draft addendum includes both SCC and onshore options.

Can you confirm whether Evergreen needs full onshore-only processing or SCCs are acceptable? That will determine the addendum language we finalize. Also, do you require an explicit clause for audit sampling frequency?

If it's helpful, I can schedule a 30-minute follow-up today to walk through the attached materials.

Best,
Ben Carter
Head of Sales — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0187

Alice —

Thanks for the checklist and for the timeline heads-up. Attached are the updated materials you requested: the subprocessor inventory, our SOC 2 attestation (redacted), the DPA addendum draft, and a short DPIA support statement. Highlights below so you can pass to legal/compliance quickly: 

- Subprocessors: included country and role columns; highlighted three infra providers that handle ephemeral caching/logs. We flag those for your review.
- Audit rights: our standard position is remote evidence within 5–10 business days; on-site audits are supported with 30 days' notice and scoped to relevant systems. The addendum draft includes both options and a sample sampling cadence clause (quarterly sampling for high-risk processors). If Evergreen prefers a fixed onshore-only clause we can supply wording and pricing for a dedicated instance.
- Transfers / Article 28/30: draft addendum includes SCCs and an Art.28 summary (processor obligations + subprocessors list placeholder). We can provide an extract for Article 30 processing activities on request; Priyom included a short one-pager for your DPIA.
- DPIA support: attached "DPIA-support_evgbank_20270409.pdf" (one page) with processing categories, dataflow summary, mitigations, and a named contact (priyom.das@redwood.com) to coordinate evidence.

Next steps we suggest:
1) Legal: review attached addendum and comment via DocuSign link (see below)
2) Compliance: confirm SCCs vs onshore requirement; if SCCs acceptable, confirm whether you want sampling cadence adjusted
3) Scheduling: if you want on-site evidence or deeper artifact review, tell us preferred windows and we'll loop in ComplianceOps

HubSpot deal: https://hubspot.com/deals/EVB-20270407

- Evidence readiness (artifact types and timelines)

- Audit logs: retention, access controls, export
- SSO and provisioning (SCIM / SAML) and org-level controls

Jordan

title: Equinox-XL integration request — compat checklist + temporary model freeze
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Raymond <alex@equinox.ai>, Elyse Navarro <elyse@bigretail.co>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 5 Apr 2027 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: Equinox-XL integration request — compat checklist + temporary model freeze

Hi Alex / Elyse,

Thanks again for sending over the initial artifact. We kicked off an internal intake with infra and runtime yesterday and I wanted to loop in the product and eng owners for a quick alignment. Two asks from our side to avoid last-minute regressions during the onboarding window:

1) Can Equinox provide a short compatibility checklist (hardware kernel targets, expected max-sequence, KV cache footprint for 8k context) and an explicit list of variants that are API-compatible with the 8B client we serve today?

2) Requesting a temporary model \"freeze\" window while we run our E2E integration tests and perf validation. Proposed window: 2027-04-12 00:00 UTC — 2027-04-16 23:59 UTC. During the window we ask that no new public variants be pushed to the artifact registry for the Equinox-XL tag used by Elyse's environment.

Monica / Dev — can you confirm the exact telemetry and regression checks we will run so I can communicate requirements to the Equinox team?

Elyse — we'll coordinate the validation tests with you once we have the checklist. Ideally we want alignment on the fallback policy if we see a perf or tokenization regression.

Attaching the initial compatibility checklist template we will use; Alex mentioned a binary artifact will follow.

Thanks,
Ben

--
Ben Carter
Head of Partnerships, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

---

From: Alex Raymond <alex@equinox.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Elyse Navarro <elyse@bigretail.co>
Date: Mon, 5 Apr 2027 13:48:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Equinox-XL integration request — compat checklist + temporary model freeze

Thanks Ben — appreciate the clear asks. We're attaching the v0.9 artifact (equinox-xl-v0.9.tar.gz) and a short compatibility matrix that answers the kernel/sequence/KV questions. Quick notes:

- v0.9 is the release we'd like you to validate. There are internal variants for int8/int4 quant that are functionally equivalent, but they have different memory layout; we'll flag which ones are binary-compatible with your runtime in the matrix.
- We can hold the public tag update to 2027-04-17 00:00 UTC to support your freeze window.

I'll also add a small README into the artifact with expected perf numbers on our test infra. Let me know if you need a sanitized trace (we can provide).

Alex
Principal Engineer, Equinox AI
alex@equinox.ai

Attachment: equinox-xl-v0.9.tar.gz (model artifact)

---

From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 6 Apr 2027 09:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Equinox-XL integration request — compat checklist + temporary model freeze

Ben — tagging in the test plan. High level overview of what we'll run during the freeze window:

- Functional: tokenization/ID mapping checks using the Equinox sample prompts (coverage: English + es + zh)
- Perf: p50/p95 latency under our continuous-batch load profile (batch sizes 1, 4, 16) on A100-80GB and RTX-8000 staging nodes
- Memory: KV growth and peak resident set over 8k context with synthetic traces
- Accuracy smoke: 100 canonical prompts, BLEU/ROUGE-like checks for deterministic tasks and soft checks for sampling tasks; flag any >5% deviation on deterministic prompts
- Telemetry: token-level latency, OOM events, kernel fallback counts, quant-decoding mismatches

We will instrument the above and post results to the Drive folder. If anything fails we'll run a rollback to the last verified variant and open JIRA-INF-3421 with failure details.

I attached the compatibility checklist template and a short rollout runbook (how we switch traffic and rollback).

—Monica
Senior Product Manager, Model Ops
monica.patel@redwood.ai

Attachments: compatibility-checklist.xlsx, rollout-runbook.md

---

From: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ethan Park <ethan.park@redwood.ai>, Alex Raymond <alex@equinox.ai>
Date: Wed, 7 Apr 2027 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Equinox-XL integration request — compat checklist + temporary model freeze

Quick technical clarifications from runtime:

- Please confirm whether Equinox v0.9 includes a custom fused attention kernel. If yes, we need the kernel selection hints (or a fallback path).
- For quant variants: confirm whether the int4 variant uses per-channel scales or per-tensor; per-channel will require a minor change to our loader.
- We will run the KV checkpoint test (writes/reads) against a 6-hour synthetic replay to validate cache correctness.

If Alex can confirm the kernel/quant details by EOD Tuesday (2027-04-07), we'll be able to finalize the test harness and sign-off the freeze window.

Dev
Senior Software Engineer, Serving Runtime
dev.patel@redwood.ai

---

From: Alex Raymond <alex@equinox.ai>
To: Dev Patel <dev.patel@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Elyse Navarro <elyse@bigretail.co>
Date: Thu, 8 Apr 2027 16:23:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Equinox-XL integration request — compat checklist + temporary model freeze

Hi all — confirming answers inline:

- Fused kernel: v0.9 does include an optional fused attention kernel. We've included a fallback CPU/kernel implementation in the artifact. We'll add kernel selection hints to the README by EOD.
- Int4 quant: uses per-channel scales. We understand that requires a loader change. I'll include a small patch snippet in the Drive folder so your loader team can preview.
- We'll hold public updates to the Equinox-XL tag until 2027-04-17 00:00 UTC as requested.

I've uploaded the README and the small loader patch to the shared Drive folder: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c4EQUINOX-INTEGRATION. Alex also created JIRA-INF-3421 for the onboarding run with the acceptance criteria.

If you want, we can schedule a 30m working session tomorrow to walk the loader patch and perf hints.

Thanks,
Alex
Principal Engineer, Equinox AI

title: Dedicated deployment: location safeguards and ephemeral cache handling
preview:
From: Natalie Cho <natalie.cho@summitcommerce.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: ops@summitcommerce.com
Date: Wed, 07 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Questions on region pinning + ephemeral caches for Dedicated

Hi Ben,

Quick follow-up from our security review: we need clarity on three items before procurement can sign off for the dedicated cluster:

1) If we pin a deployment to us-west-2, can any persistent snapshot or backup ever be copied outside that region?
2) For the inference runtime, you mentioned an "ephemeral KV cache" — does any portion of that cache get paged to durable block storage or a snapshot that could cross regions?
3) Retention: what are the default retention windows for service-generated logs (request logs, audit logs) on Dedicated, and can we shorten them per account?

We have an internal deadline Friday to get the answers into the compliance packet. Appreciate concise, actionable responses + any artifacts.

Thanks,
Natalie
Director, Cloud Security
SummitCommerce


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Natalie Cho <natalie.cho@summitcommerce.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 07 Apr 2027 10:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Questions on region pinning + ephemeral caches for Dedicated

Hi Natalie — thanks for sending these; short answers below and Karthik + Laura are copying in for details/artifacts.

1) Snapshots/backups: By default, persistent block snapshots tied to a pinned Dedicated cluster are stored in the same region (us-west-2). We do not replicate persistent snapshots to other regions unless the customer explicitly enables cross-region replication for disaster recovery (that is a separate opt-in feature with explicit contract language).

2) Ephemeral KV cache: The runtime's KV cache is memory-first and kept in-GPU/host RAM. We do have a mechanism to page cold segments to ephemeral local SSDs on the node for memory pressure; those SSD writes are short-lived, encrypted at rest, and cleared on pod termination. Importantly, those ephemeral disks are not included in persistent snapshot sets and are not copied to remote object storage as part of normal operation.

3) Service logs: Default retention for request logs is 90 days in the customer tenant (shorter retention possible via config). Audit logs are stored for 365 days by default for Dedicated (enterprise tier); we can configure shorter windows (e.g., 30/90/180 days) as a contractual setting.

I'll attach a short ops playbook and our cache handling matrix. Laura: can you attach the official attestation and the backup policy excerpt? Karthik: can you confirm the SSD wipe timeline and whether we provide a signed wipe cert for the tenant?

Next steps: we'll send a one-page attestation + redline language you can drop in the procurement packet.

Best,
Ben
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Natalie Cho <natalie.cho@summitcommerce.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 07 Apr 2027 13:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Questions on region pinning + ephemeral caches for Dedicated

Natalie — attaching the formal artifacts you requested:

- redwood-dedicated-dataflow-diagram.pdf (high-level map: compute nodes, ephemeral SSD, snapshot boundaries)
- cache-to-disk-policy.csv (matrix showing what can be paged, retention of paged segments, wipe triggers)
- dedicated-backup-policy-excerpt.txt (legal summary of snapshot scope and cross-region replication opt-in language)

Key legal points for procurement:
- Cross-region snapshot replication is explicit opt-in and requires a separate DR configuration and service addendum.
- Ephemeral local SSD writes are treated as non-persistent and are covered by our wipe/eviction SLA (wipe begins on node termination and completes within 120 seconds for page file metadata, full scrub of SSD lane within 24 hours). We can provide a signed scrub log if requested as part of an annual attestation.
- We can include a clause limiting backups/snapshot exports to the pinned region unless SummitCommerce requests DR replication.

If you'd like, I can schedule a 20-minute call with our security operations engineer to walk through the artifacts.

Regards,
Laura Bennett
Head of Trust & Security Operations | Redwood Inference


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Natalie Cho <natalie.cho@summitcommerce.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 08 Apr 2027 15:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Questions on region pinning + ephemeral caches for Dedicated

Hi Natalie — quick technical clarifications to add to Laura and Ben's notes:

- The runtime's KV cache is strictly bounded by tenant namespaces. When paging occurs we write encrypted blocks to the node-local NVMe under a per-tenant key. Those local NVMe blocks are never uploaded to object storage and are wiped on pod termination.
- Wipe mechanics: on pod termination we zero metadata pointers immediately; a background scrub erases the NVMe lanes. We keep a per-tenant wipe log (transient) to support attestations; those logs are rotated and purged per customer retention settings. If you require a signed wipe certificate after deprovision, we can provide that as part of the account offboarding flow (audit-led; delivery within 3 business days).

- Logs: request traces are kept per-tenant; you can choose in the Dedicated config whether traces are retained locally only or exported to your S3-compatible bucket (export is opt-in). If exported, that export follows your configured bucket lifecycle policies.

I'll also note a small caveat: if you enable cross-region DR or make explicit exports (backup exports, log exports), those objects will follow the export destination rules; by default we do NOT export unless configured.

Happy to hop on a call if you want a short demo of the wipe + cache lifecycle steps.

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Senior Engineer, Inference Runtime | Redwood Inference

> 2) Can you confirm the turnaround for a signed wipe cert during account offboarding?

>
> —Natalie

Subject: Questions on region pinning + ephemeral caches for Dedicated

Hi Ben,

Quick follow-up from our security review: we need clarity on three items before procurement can sign off for the dedicated cluster:

1) If we pin a deployment to us-west-2, can any persistent snapshot or backup ever be copied outside that region?
2) For the inference runtime, you mentioned an "ephemeral KV cache" — does any portion of that cache get paged to durable block storage or a snapshot that could cross regions?
3) Retention: what are the default retention windows for service-generated logs (request logs, audit logs) on Dedicated, and can we shorten them per account?

We have an internal deadline Friday to get the answers into the compliance packet. Appreciate concise, actionable responses + any artifacts.

Thanks,
Natalie
Director, Cloud Security
SummitCommerce


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Natalie Cho <natalie.cho@summitcommerce.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 07 Apr 2027 10:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Questions on region pinning + ephemeral caches for Dedicated

Hi Natalie — thanks for sending these; short answers below and Karthik + Laura are copying in for details/artifacts.

1) Snapshots/backups: By default, persistent block snapshots tied to a pinned Dedicated cluster are stored in the same region (us-west-2). We do not replicate persistent snapshots to other regions unless the customer explicitly enables cross-region replication for disaster recovery (that is a separate opt-in feature with explicit contract language).

2) Ephemeral KV cache: The runtime's KV cache is memory-first and kept in-GPU/host RAM. We do have a mechanism to page cold segments to ephemeral local SSDs on the node for memory pressure; those SSD writes are short-lived, encrypted at rest, and cleared on pod termination. Importantly, those ephemeral disks are not included in persistent snapshot sets and are not copied to remote object storage as part of normal operation.

3) Service logs: Default retention for request logs is 90 days in the customer tenant (shorter retention possible via config). Audit logs are stored for 365 days by default for Dedicated (enterprise tier); we can configure shorter windows (e.g., 30/90/180 days) as a contractual setting.

I'll attach a short ops playbook and our cache handling matrix. Laura: can you attach the official attestation and the backup policy excerpt? Karthik: can you confirm the SSD wipe timeline and whether we provide a signed wipe cert for the tenant?

Next steps: we'll send a one-page attestation + redline language you can drop in the procurement packet.

Best,
Ben
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference

Key legal points for procurement:
- Cross-region snapshot replication is explicit opt-in and requires a separate DR configuration and service addendum.

title: Hosted API: latency measurement methodology and evidence package
preview:
From: Sam Torres <s.torres@evergreenhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement <procurement@evergreenhealth.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Clarification on Hosted API latency measurement for SLA

Hi Ben —

Thanks again for the Hosted API SLA draft. Before our procurement review next Wednesday we need a concise description of how Redwood measures the p50/p95 values you commit to. Specifically:

- Which endpoint(s) are measured (edge vs origin)?
- Is p95 computed on successful requests only or across all responses?
- How are retries and client-side timeouts handled?
- What warmup/carveout windows do you apply (deployments, canaries, regional maintenance)?
- What sampling frequency do you use for high-volume customers?

We also need an evidence package you can share for audit: sample raw traces, a small CSV of sampled request latencies, and a short doc describing the aggregation windows and any exclusion logic.

Could you send a one-page method summary + the requested artifacts? Preferably PDFs / CSVs we can drop into our vendor risk folder.

Appreciate it — we want to avoid ambiguous wording in the contract.

—Sam
Sam Torres
SRE Manager, Evergreen Health
Attach: latency_sample_queries.csv (requested)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Sam Torres <s.torres@evergreenhealth.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Apr 2027 13:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Clarification on Hosted API latency measurement for SLA

Sam —

Thanks for the clear list. Attached is our draft methodology (redwood_latency_methodology_v1.pdf) and a small CSV extract of sampled requests (latency_sample_queries.csv). Summary below; I kept it intentionally concrete so it can be pasted into the SLA.

Summary (to be included in contract):
- Measurement target: latency for text generation endpoints at api.redwood.com/v1/respond (production edge). We distinguish edge-observed latency (client-to-edge) vs origin processing; SLA offers edge-observed p50/p95.
- Successful-only calculation: p50 and p95 are computed over successful responses (HTTP 200-series). Failed requests (5xx) are excluded from percentile math but counted in availability metrics.
- Retries/timeouts: Client-side retries are de-duped by request ID where available. Server-generated retries (internally retried by gateway) have final-response latency recorded. Timeouts that elicit no response are excluded from percentile calculation; they are reflected in availability/uptime SLOs.
- Windowing & aggregation: We use rolling 1-hour windows sampled every 5 minutes for operational monitoring; contractual measurement for monthly SLO evaluation is based on daily aggregation into calendar-day buckets, and percentiles are computed on the union of daily buckets for the billing month.
- Warmups/carveouts: Automatic 10-minute warmup carveout after canary cutover; scheduled maintenance windows >5 minutes are excluded when customers are notified with a maintenance ticket. For unscheduled incidents, audit evidence will include incident timelines and affected buckets.
- Sampling: For customers >10k reqs/min we maintain systematic sampling at 1% across all request paths, stratified by region. For lower-volume customers we use full-sample measurements. Sampling parameters and sample seed are provided in the evidence package for verification.

Attached items:
- redwood_latency_methodology_v1.pdf (method and definitions)
- latency_sample_queries.csv (sanitized sample of 1,000 rows)
- evidence_snapshot_20270407.tar.gz (contains anonymized traces and sampling seed)

If you want explicit contract language I can propose a short clause that replaces the generic p50/p95 line. Happy to hop on a 20-min call if you want us to walk through the evidence data.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference
Attach: redwood_latency_methodology_v1.pdf, latency_sample_queries.csv, evidence_snapshot_20270407.tar.gz

Date: Wed, 7 Apr 2027 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: wording tweaks for Evergreen method doc

Ben / Connor —

Quick read of Ben's draft. A few suggested edits we should incorporate before sending to procurement:

- Replace 'successful responses' with 'successful, non-provisioning responses (HTTP 200-299, excluding 202 polling semantics)' to avoid ambiguity with async endpoints.
- Add explicit statement that p95 excludes synthetic health-check traffic and internal observability probes.
- Call out that sampling seed + extraction script will be provided for reproducibility (this helps their audit team).
- For warmup carveouts, list the canary ID and GC cutoff thresholds we use for auto-carveout (10 minutes as you noted).

Also suggest we include a one-paragraph fallback statement: if a regional edge is unavailable, we will route to the nearest region and the latency commitment will be measured against the actual edge that served the request (we should note regional fallbacks in the SLA).

I'll mark up the PDF and push a v1.1. Connor, can you confirm the sampling seed is exportable and that the evidence tar contains the minimal PII?

—Lauren
Lauren Bishop
Head of Customer Reliability, Redwood Inference

> > Sam —
> >
> > Thanks for the clear list. Attached is our draft methodology (redwood_latency_methodology_v1.pdf) and a small CSV extract of sampled requests (latency_sample_queries.csv). Summary below; I kept it intentionally concrete so it can be pasted into the SLA.
> >
> > [snip summary]
> >
> > —Ben


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Sam Torres <s.torres@evergreenhealth.com>, Procurement <procurement@evergreenhealth.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Apr 2027 17:36:00 -0700
Subject: Final: Hosted API latency measurement & evidence (Evergreen Health)

Sam / Procurement team —

Per our thread and internal review, please find the final measurement summary and evidence bundle attached. Key clarifications incorporated from your ask and our internal review:

1) Scope & endpoints: SLA p50/p95 refer to edge-observed latency for production text-generation endpoints (api.redwood.com/v1/respond). If Evergreen routes to a regional fallback, the latency measurement is for the edge that ultimately served the request.

2) Percentile computation: p50/p95 are calculated over successful, non-provisioning responses (HTTP 200-299). Failed requests are not included in percentile math (they are counted against availability).

3) Retries & timeouts: Any client-side retry with a new request ID is treated as a separate request; server-internal retries are collapsed and the final-response timestamp is used. Timeouts without responses are recorded under availability.

4) Aggregation policy: Operational monitoring uses rolling 1-hour windows sampled every 5 minutes. Contractual monthly evaluation aggregates daily buckets for the billing month; percentiles are computed on the union of those daily buckets.

5) Warmup / carveouts: 10-minute automatic warmup carveout after canary cutover; scheduled maintenance >5 minutes is excluded if pre-notified. Unscheduled incidents will include an evidence timeline.

6) Sampling & reproducibility: For high-volume customers (>10k reqs/min) we provide a 1% stratified sample and a reproducibility package (seed + extraction script) in the evidence tarball. For lower-volume customers we provide full-sample data.

Attachments in this message: redwood_latency_methodology_v1.pdf (final, marked v1.1), latency_sample_queries.csv (sanitized sample), evidence_snapshot_20270407.tar.gz (anonymized traces + sampling seed + extraction script).

If this looks good I can prepare a short contract clause for insertion. If procurement wants an auditor-run validation, we can schedule a controlled sample export session next week.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Director, Solutions — Redwood Inference
Phone: +1 415 555 0117
ben_carter@redwood.com
Attach: redwood_latency_methodology_v1.pdf, latency_sample_queries.csv, evidence_snapshot_20270407.tar.gz

title: Private connectivity & on-prem bundle — implementation playbook
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena@heliostx.com>, Marco Santoro <marco.santoro@heliostx.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 05 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Private connectivity & on-prem bundle — implementation playbook

Hi Elena / Marco —

Thanks again for choosing Redwood for the private deployment. As discussed on the call last week, I wanted to kick off the implementation playbook so your network and security teams have a single reference. Below is the proposed scope and immediate asks so we can lock dates for the on-prem package install and VPC peering validation.

Scope (short):
- Private connectivity: AWS VPC peering (primary), with optional VPN fallback for on-prem.
- Private endpoints for control plane APIs and telemetry collectors.
- On-prem package: installer tarball + hardware checklist (for air-gapped sites).
- Key & secret management integration (KMS/HSM).

Immediate asks from Helios networking/security team:
1) Confirm the AWS account IDs we should peer with and which VPC/subnet will accept the peering (attach route table CIDR if possible).
2) Provide the public IP range for the NAT/transit gateway if you expect to use egress rules for telemetry.
3) Indicate whether you will require site-to-site VPN as primary or only as fallback.
4) Point of contact for HSM/KMS and any required certificates.

Proposed timeline (workable draft):
- This week (Apr 5–9): Exchange account IDs & network CIDRs; Redwood shares on-prem package spec.
- Week of Apr 12: Network validation & peering test in staging.
- Week of Apr 19: On-prem package installation window (2–4 hours), post-install smoke tests.

Attachments: redwood-onprem-package-spec.pdf (installer details), sample network diagram.

If the above looks good, can you confirm the two primary points of contact and whether Apr 19–23 works for the install window?

Thanks — I’ll coordinate SOW finalization once the timeline is agreed.

Best,
Ava Chen
Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood-onprem-package-spec.pdf (PDF, 1.2MB)]



From: Elena Ruiz <elena@heliostx.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marco Santoro <marco.santoro@heliostx.com>, GCP Networks <networking@heliostx.com>
Date: Mon, 05 Apr 2027 13:01:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Private connectivity & on-prem bundle — implementation playbook

Ava —

Thanks for the clear plan. We're aligned on scope. Quick answers below and I've attached our current IP allocation sheet for the project.

Answers:
1) AWS Account for peering: 987654321000 (primary). Peer to VPC vpc-0a1b2c3d (CIDR 10.48.0.0/16).
2) Egress/NAT ranges: 34.201.12.0/24 (production egress).
3) VPN: we prefer VPC peering as primary. VPN only if peering causes routing conflicts during staging.
4) HSM: Contact is Marco Santoro (cc'd). He manages our Key Broker and will share the cert chain.

POCs:

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Kai Müller <kai.mueller@bellwether.de>, Alicia Chen <alicia.chen@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-04-06T16:05:00+02:00
Subject: Re: Bellwether x Acme - demo invite

Thanks Kai — quick heads up: when Alicia forwarded the invite into our APAC test account it showed up as a "floating" time (no timezone anchor) and converted to the recipient's local clock incorrectly. APAC participants in Singapore/Japan then saw the meeting at 02:00 the following day instead of 17:00 SGT.

Can you resend the invite anchored to a timezone/UTC (i.e., include DTSTART with TZID or a clear UTC timestamp) instead of a floating/local-time-only event? That prevents calendar apps from interpreting it relative to each recipient's calendar locale.

Proposed workaround for the short term: we should confirm by UTC — if the meeting is 08:00 UTC on 2027-04-14, that maps to 10:00 CEST and 18:00 SGT. If that mapping is correct, Kai can reissue an anchored ICS and Alicia can re-forward.

Marissa / Karthik — can you try importing bellwether-invite.ics into a sandbox calendar and paste what DTSTART line shows? That'll help us diagnose whether this is the scheduling system exporting floating times.

Thanks,
Ben
Senior Sales Manager, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Kai Müller <kai.mueller@bellwether.de>, Alicia Chen <alicia.chen@acme-corp.com>
Date: 2027-04-06T16:22:00+02:00
Subject: Re: Bellwether x Acme - demo invite

On Apr 6, 2027, Ben Carter wrote:
> Can you resend the invite anchored to a timezone/UTC...

I imported the attached bellwether-invite.ics into a disposable Google calendar and also opened it in a plain-text editor. The DTSTART in the file is "20270414T100000" (no TZID, no trailing Z). That's a floating time. When Google sees that and a recipient's calendar is set to Singapore, it interprets it as 10:00 SGT (which is 02:00 UTC), not 08:00 UTC. So this explains the 02:00-issue.

Karthik, quick ask: can you reply with a sample iCal snippet that shows the preferred fields for an anchored invite (we want either DTSTART:20270414T080000Z or DTSTART;TZID=Europe/Berlin:20270414T100000). I'll paste that back to Kai.

Also marking for follow-up: add a note to our internal troubleshooting doc that forwarded invites from third-party schedulers sometimes strip TZID and default to floating times.

Cheers,
Marissa
Senior Sales Ops, Redwood Inference


From: Kai Müller <kai.mueller@bellwether.de>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Alicia Chen <alicia.chen@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-04-06T20:30:00+02:00
Subject: Re: Bellwether x Acme - demo invite

Thanks all — apologies for the headache. Our scheduling vendor exports floating DTSTARTs by default unless you enable the "export timezones" flag. I've toggled that and re-exported the calendar. New file attached (bellwether-invite-anchored.ics) with DTSTART:20270414T080000Z (UTC).

Can someone confirm that this shows as 10:00 CEST and 18:00 SGT on your side? I also pasted a Google Meet link into the invite so attendees have a consistent join URL.

Attachment: bellwether-invite-anchored.ics (text/ics)

Best,
Kai


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Kai Müller <kai.mueller@bellwether.de>, Alicia Chen <alicia.chen@acme-corp.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-04-07T13:45:00+02:00
Subject: Re: Bellwether x Acme - demo invite

Confirmed — I imported bellwether-invite-anchored.ics and Google shows it as 10:00 CEST / 18:00 SGT. APAC attendees report it's correctly at 18:00 local time. Thanks for the quick fix, Kai.

Alicia — when you forward the meeting to any additional external participants, please forward the anchored invite or resend from the Bellwether system with timezone export enabled. We'll update our partner-runbook with a small checklist about anchored times so this doesn't recur.

Can you resend the invite anchored to a timezone/UTC (i.e., include DTSTART with TZID or a clear UTC timestamp) instead of a floating/local-time-only event? That prevents calendar apps from interpreting it relative to each recipient's calendar locale.

Proposed workaround for the short term: we should confirm by UTC — if the meeting is 08:00 UTC on 2027-04-14, that maps to 10:00 CEST and 18:00 SGT. If that mapping is correct, Kai can reissue an anchored ICS and Alicia can re-forward.

Marissa / Karthik — can you try importing bellwether-invite.ics into a sandbox calendar and paste what DTSTART line shows? That'll help us diagnose whether this is the scheduling system exporting floating times.

Thanks,
Ben
Senior Sales Manager, Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Kai Müller <kai.mueller@bellwether.de>, Alicia Chen <alicia.chen@acme-corp.com>
Date: 2027-04-06T16:22:00+02:00
Subject: Re: Bellwether x Acme - demo invite

On Apr 6, 2027, Ben Carter wrote:
> Can you resend the invite anchored to a timezone/UTC...

I imported the attached bellwether-invite.ics into a disposable Google calendar and also opened it in a plain-text editor. The DTSTART in the file is "20270414T100000" (no TZID, no trailing Z). That's a floating time. When Google sees that and a recipient's calendar is set to Singapore, it interprets it as 10:00 SGT (which is 02:00 UTC), not 08:00 UTC. So this explains the 02:00-issue.

Karthik, quick ask: can you reply with a sample iCal snippet that shows the preferred fields for an anchored invite (we want either DTSTART:20270414T080000Z or DTSTART;TZID=Europe/Berlin:20270414T100000). I'll paste that back to Kai.

Also marking for follow-up: add a note to our internal troubleshooting doc that forwarded invites from third-party schedulers sometimes strip TZID and default to floating times.

Cheers,
Marissa
Senior Sales Ops, Redwood Inference

title: Mistral Health renewal — proposed commit shift & term amendment
preview:
From: Sandra Kim <sandra.kim@mistralhealth.com>
To: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 30 Mar 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Renewal discussion + updated 12mo forecast

Hi Ethan,

We’re kicking off our renewal planning for Mistral Health and I wanted to flag a few items up front so we can make the next quarter tidy: 1) our usage is likely to increase ~25% in Q3 due to a docs ingestion project; 2) procurement needs a clear ceiling/price-hold for first 6 months; and 3) we’re open to re-structuring the commit if there’s a credit/discount trade that makes sense.

Can you send over a short set of options (3 paths) that show: commit levels, net effective rates, any upfront credits, and what a 6-month price-hold would look like? Also include any exclusions (e.g., Private deployments) and how bursting over commit would be handled.

We’ll pull in Ben Owens and our procurement inbox to review. Happy to do a 30-min sync next week.

Thanks,
Sandra Kim
Head of ML Platform
Mistral Health
attachments: forecast_Q3_2027.csv (spreadsheet stub)

From: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.com>
To: Sandra Kim <sandra.kim@mistralhealth.com>, Ben Owens <benjamin.owens@mistralhealth.com>
Cc: procurement@mistralhealth.com
Date: Wed, 31 Mar 2027 10:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Renewal discussion + updated 12mo forecast

Thanks Sandra — appreciate the heads up. Attached is a short package with three alternative renewal paths and a commit calculator so you can toggle the uplift. High level:

Option A — Moderate uplift (recommended)- 12-month commit + 25% uplift vs current baseline- 10% off list rates year 1 (applied as billing credits across first 3 months)- 6-month price-hold on base token price; burst bill at standard overage rates- No coverage for Private/on-prem consumption (must be quoted separately)

Option B — Aggressive commit (best unit economics)- 18-month commit + 40% uplift- 18% effective discount in year 1 (one-time prepaid credit applied monthly)- Price-hold for 9 months- Includes limited burst pool (configured as reserved burst capacity)

Option C — Low-commit safety net- 12-month with only 10% uplift- 5% discount + $30k credit bucket to offset overages- 6-month price-hold limited to hosted API only

Notes: all options assume current Mistral model variants. We’ve excluded Private deployments from the price-hold because of different hosting cost profiles and auditing needs. I’ve attached a redlineable term sheet (v3) and the commit calculator.

If any of the numbers look promising, I can schedule a 30-min call with Marissa and Vivek to quickly align on what concessions we can approve from Regional Sales vs. what needs a CS/Finance escalation. Would Thursday 10am PT work for you?

Best,
Ethan Park
Account Executive — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
attachments: Mistral_Renewal_Term_Sheet_v3.pdf; Redwood-Commit-Calculator.xlsx

From: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 1 Apr 2027 08:17:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Mistral renewal — proposed commit shift & term amendment (need quick decision)

Marissa/Vivek — forwarding Sandra’s ask and the options I sent. Quick summary and ask:

Context: Mistral is a mid-market healthcare customer with predictable growth and strict procurement windows. They want a 6-month price-hold and are willing to increase commit if we provide credits/discounts. Usage forecast attached suggests a Q3 spike of ~25%.

What I’m proposing we can approve at regional level (no legal change):

1) Offer Option A as the baseline (12m, +25% uplift) with 10% year-1 discount applied as a $90k credit bucket spread over first 90 days.
2) Include a firm 6-month price-hold only for hosted API, with explicit exclusion clause for Private/on-prem – we’ll add an audit right if they push.
3) Add a simple burst policy: overage billed at published overage rate unless they auto-scale into burst pool; any sustained >120% of commit for 2 months triggers review.

What needs escalation to Finance/CS:
- Option B (18% effective discount + 9-month price-hold) — needs finance approval because of prepaid credit mechanics.

Approval ask: Can you sign off on Option A as a delegated concession (up to $100k total credits) and confirm the language we should use for the Private deployment carve-out? If yes I’ll send amended term sheet and a DocuSign early next week.

Quoted from Sandra’s original: "> procurement needs a clear ceiling/price-hold for first 6 months; and we’re open to re-structuring the commit if there’s a credit/discount trade that makes sense."

Thanks — quick thumbs up would be great.

—Ethan

From: Ethan Park <ethan_park@redwood.com>
To: Sandra Kim <sandra.kim@mistralhealth.com>, Ben Owens <benjamin.owens@mistralhealth.com>, procurement@mistralhealth.com
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek_kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 6 Apr 2027 15:42:00 -0700
Subject: Final — amended term sheet & next steps (DocuSign link)

Sandra / Ben — thanks for the call last week. Per our conversation we are aligned on the Option A path: 12-month commitment with a 25% uplift and a $90k credit applied across the first 90 days (appears as billing credits). Key points summarized below: 

- Commit: 25% uplift vs current baseline for 12 months- Credits: $90,000 applied as bill credits across months 1–3- Price-hold: 6 months on hosted API pricing; Private/on-prem excluded and billed per a separate quote- Burst: overage at published overage rates; sustained >120% of commit for two consecutive months will trigger a capacity review and proposed restructure- Term sheet attached (Mistral_Renewal_Term_Sheet_v3.pdf) and commit calculator included

I’ve attached the redline and sent the DocuSign envelope here: https://docusign.com/envelope/ABC123456789 — please review and sign by Friday to lock the price-hold window. If procurement needs a revised PO flow we can include that as an appendix (procurement@mistralhealth.com).

If you’d like the Option B economics pulled for internal review, we can do that, but it will need finance sign-off.

Thanks again — looking forward to continuing the partnership.

Best,
Ethan Park
Account Executive — Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference
attachments: Mistral_Renewal_Term_Sheet_v3.pdf; Redwood-Commit-Calculator.xlsx

3) Add a simple burst policy: overage billed at published overage rate unless they auto-scale into burst pool; any sustained >120% of commit for 2 months triggers review.

What needs escalation to Finance/CS:
- Option B (18% effective discount + 9-month price-hold) — needs finance approval because of prepaid credit mechanics.

Approval ask: Can you sign off on Option A as a delegated concession (up to $100k total credits) and confirm the language we should use for the Private deployment carve-out? If yes I’ll send amended term sheet and a DocuSign early next week.

Quoted from Sandra’s original: "> procurement needs a clear ceiling/price-hold for first 6 months; and we’re open to re-structuring the commit if there’s a credit/discount trade that makes sense."

Thanks — quick thumbs up would be great.

—Ethan

title: Dedicated capacity options for inference — pilot & staged activation
preview:
From: Claire Park <claire.park@nimblehealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Alex Jensen <ajensen@nimblehealth.com>
Date: Mon, 29 Mar 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Dedicated capacity options for inference (pilot & staged activation)

Hi Ava,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. We're lining up the architecture and procurement plan for an inference product (clinical decision support) and need to understand Redwood's dedicated deployment options. Key things we care about:

- Recommended cluster sizing for a 95th percentile concurrency of ~2.5k reqs/min with burst to 4k/min for 30–60s windows
- Minimum live/reserved GPUs required to keep KV cache efficiency and latency SLOs (p99 target ~250ms)
- A safe ramp schedule (pilot -> growth -> steady state) that balances cost and warm-up risk
- Any warm-pool / pre-warming strategies and how they’re billed

We are procurement-led, so visibility on pricing bands (pilot credits, minimum committed lanes, and discount thresholds) would help. We are in the US region and hoping to kick a pilot in early April.

Can you send a few concrete options (cluster sizes, min GPUs, ramp timeline) plus any operational notes about cache/warm-up behavior?

Thanks,
Claire
Claire Park
Head of Platform Partnerships
Nimble Health



From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Claire Park <claire.park@nimblehealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 30 Mar 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity options for inference (pilot & staged activation)

Hi Claire —

Appreciate the details. Attached are three pragmatic activation pathways tailored to the traffic profile you shared. High-level summary below; detailed pricing & timeline is in nimble-dedicated-options-v1.pdf and activation-timeline.xlsx (attached).

Option A — Short pilot (conservative)
- Pilot footprint: 8 GPU-equivalent lanes (A100/80GB-equivalent) for a 30-day pilot
- Min reserved: 8 (pilot-only) -> billed as reserved for the pilot window
- Ramp plan: pilot (M0) for 30d -> optional expand to 16 GPUs in month 2 if latency/throughput validated
- Use-case: validate prompt sets, KV cache hit rate, and initial failover behavior

Option B — Staged growth (recommended for predictable production)
- Pilot: 12 GPUs for 30 days (validation + 10% headroom)
- Minimum committed: 24 GPUs after month 3 (reserved), with autoscale to burst lanes up to 48 for traffic spikes
- Ramp schedule: 12 (pilot) -> 24 (month 3) -> 48 (month 6)
- Warm-pool: 10% idle warm pool retained inside the committed pool to preserve KV cache and reduce cold-start latency

Option C — Aggressive committed posture (for performance-critical products)
- Immediate reserved baseline: 48 GPUs, min reserved 24 during the first 60 days while we tune models
- Ramp to 96 GPUs by month 9 as traffic and models scale
- Best for customers who want strict latency SLOs and capacity predictability

Operational notes
- KV cache effectiveness: We recommend at least 12–16 GPUs active to get stable cache hit ratios for long-context models; very small pilots (<=8 GPUs) can have lower steady-state cache hit rates and higher cold-token cost.
- Autoscaling: available but it’s subject to warm-up delays for very large model weights; we recommend burst lanes for traffic spikes rather than relying on instantaneous scale-to-zero.
- Billing: reserved GPUs billed monthly; warm-pool usage is billed as part of the reserved seat. Burst lanes are billed at an overage/burst rate.

We can include a 30-day pilot credit and a staged discount schedule (5% at 12 GPUs reserved for 6 months, 12% at 24+ for 12 months) — will confirm with finance once you indicate a preferred path.

If you like Option B we can propose a concrete month-by-month activation plan and draft terms.

Best,
Ava Chen
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

Attachments: nimble-dedicated-options-v1.pdf (proposal), activation-timeline.xlsx (timeline matrix)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Claire Park <claire.park@nimblehealth.com>, Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 31 Mar 2027 08:47:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity options for inference (pilot & staged activation)

Claire — quick engineering notes to complement Ava's options below.

1) Min live GPUs and cache behavior
- For large context LLMs (8k+ tokens), we see cache warm-up curves improve significantly once you have ~12–16 concurrently live lanes serving the same model family. Below that, the working set frequently misses and you pay more in token compute.

2) Autoscaling & pre-warm strategy
- We suggest a small warm pool (10–15% of reserved) to keep KV shards resident. The warm pool is charged as reserved capacity but avoids the latency spikes of cold starts.

3) Model sharding and memory footprint
- If you plan to run multiple model sizes, plan for headroom: e.g., one 8xA100 cluster can host ~3x medium-sized models before memory pressure forces extra nodes.

4) SLA and 99th-percentile latency
- We target 99th < 350ms on committed clusters with warm-pool enabled; hitting 250ms p99 requires a larger reserved baseline and higher burst capacity.

Happy to join a technical call to walk the team through the warm-up telemetry and how we'll instrument cache hit-rate dashboards.

-ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>

Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity options for inference (pilot & staged activation)

Claire/Alex — thanks for the clear ask. Proposed commercial/activation compromise below (happy to refine):

- Pilot phase (30 days): 12 GPUs reserved, Redwood provides a one-time 100% credit on reserved charges for the pilot (credit applies to first 30d only).
- Validation window: at end of pilot we evaluate 2 KPIs (p99 latency & average request rate). If both targets met, Nimble escalates to the staged commit.
- Staged commit (months 2–4): increase to 24 GPUs reserved by month 3. If KPIs are not met, Nimble may choose to extend pilot for 30d or exit with a 90-day rollback right (refund/credit mechanics defined in SOW).
- Long-term ramp: schedule to reach 48 GPUs by month 6 contingent on usage growth; commitment discounts: 8% @ 12 GPU commit (12+ months), 14% @ 24 GPU commit (12+ months), 20% @ 48 GPU commit (12+ months).
- Early-exit and credits: 90-day operational rollback available in first 6 months; finance will model pro-rata credits for unused reserved capacity under the rollback clause.

Operationally we'll reserve a small warm pool (~10%) inside the reserved seats to preserve KV cache and reduce cold latency. We will also provide dashboard access (token-level burn, cache hit rates, per-route latency).

Hi Ava,

Thanks for the quick intro last week. We're lining up the architecture and procurement plan for an inference product (clinical decision support) and need to understand Redwood's dedicated deployment options. Key things we care about:

- Recommended cluster sizing for a 95th percentile concurrency of ~2.5k reqs/min with burst to 4k/min for 30–60s windows
- Minimum live/reserved GPUs required to keep KV cache efficiency and latency SLOs (p99 target ~250ms)
- A safe ramp schedule (pilot -> growth -> steady state) that balances cost and warm-up risk
- Any warm-pool / pre-warming strategies and how they’re billed

We are procurement-led, so visibility on pricing bands (pilot credits, minimum committed lanes, and discount thresholds) would help. We are in the US region and hoping to kick a pilot in early April.

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>

Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity options for inference (pilot & staged activation)

Hi Claire —

Appreciate the details. Attached are three pragmatic activation pathways tailored to the traffic profile you shared. High-level summary below; detailed pricing & timeline is in nimble-dedicated-options-v1.pdf and activation-timeline.xlsx (attached).

Option A — Short pilot (conservative)
- Pilot footprint: 8 GPU-equivalent lanes (A100/80GB-equivalent) for a 30-day pilot
- Min reserved: 8 (pilot-only) -> billed as reserved for the pilot window
- Ramp plan: pilot (M0) for 30d -> optional expand to 16 GPUs in month 2 if latency/throughput validated
- Use-case: validate prompt sets, KV cache hit rate, and initial failover behavior

Option B — Staged growth (recommended for predictable production)
- Pilot: 12 GPUs for 30 days (validation + 10% headroom)
- Minimum committed: 24 GPUs after month 3 (reserved), with autoscale to burst lanes up to 48 for traffic spikes
- Ramp schedule: 12 (pilot) -> 24 (month 3) -> 48 (month 6)
- Warm-pool: 10% idle warm pool retained inside the committed pool to preserve KV cache and reduce cold-start latency

Option C — Aggressive committed posture (for performance-critical products)
- Immediate reserved baseline: 48 GPUs, min reserved 24 during the first 60 days while we tune models
- Ramp to 96 GPUs by month 9 as traffic and models scale
- Best for customers who want strict latency SLOs and capacity predictability

Operational notes
- KV cache effectiveness: We recommend at least 12–16 GPUs active to get stable cache hit ratios for long-context models; very small pilots (<=8 GPUs) can have lower steady-state cache hit rates and higher cold-token cost.
- Autoscaling: available but it’s subject to warm-up delays for very large model weights; we recommend burst lanes for traffic spikes rather than relying on instantaneous scale-to-zero.
- Billing: reserved GPUs billed monthly; warm-pool usage is billed as part of the reserved seat. Burst lanes are billed at an overage/burst rate.

We can include a 30-day pilot credit and a staged discount schedule (5% at 12 GPUs reserved for 6 months, 12% at 24+ for 12 months) — will confirm with finance once you indicate a preferred path.

If you like Option B we can propose a concrete month-by-month activation plan and draft terms.

Best,
Ava Chen
Account Executive | Redwood Inference

From: Rajiv Patel <rajiv.patel@procureplus.com>

Cc: ProcurePlus Onboarding <procureplus-ops@procureplus.com>
Date: Thu, 01 Apr 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Vendor setup + MSA redline & PO field questions

Hi Camila —

Thanks for taking this on. We're prepping ProcurePlus to go live with Redwood and we have a couple of items that are gating our Ariba and Coupa activation:

1) MSA redlines: we've attached our preferred edits in the supplier questionnaire and redline packet — can legal review and flag any blocker clauses (liability cap, audit language, data retention) that would prevent procurement sign-off?

2) PO field mapping: our AR team requires the following fields on inbound POs for automated reconciliation: billTo (org ID), costCenter, accountCode, and a reference for netting/offset. Can Redwood accept netting (offsetting invoices with credits) or will we need separate invoices and manual reconciliation? Also, are there special field names Redwood expects in Ariba vs Coupa?

3) Timeline: we are targeting vendor activation the week of April 12. Is that feasible from your side? If so, please confirm the steps and the point of contact for vendor provisioning in Ariba.

Attaching: ProcurePlus_Supplier_Onboarding_Form.pdf (supply-side questionnaire)

Rajiv

Hi Rajiv —

Appreciate the clear ask. We're aligned on the main threads here: legal redline review (MSA), order-form alignment, and procurement portal field mapping (Ariba/Coupa). High-level next steps:

- Legal: Marcus will triage your redlines and call out anything that requires executive sign-off. We'll produce a single redline summary and suggested compromise table.
- Order form: I'll attach our OrderForm_Template_Ariba.xlsx for your procurement team to review; we'll map the required fields there.
